cultures.name: Imperial Heartland Culture
cultures.description: The dominant human civilization, centered on the capital Aranthem and the seven-tiered hierarchy of the High Conclave. An empire that still functions — taxes collected, roads maintained, courts adjudicated — but has lost the conviction that any of it matters. The empire governs through procedure, precedent, and the quiet assumption that since things have always worked, they will continue to. The Pallor overhead is taken as a given, like weather. The possibility that it could fail is treated as a thought experiment by scholars and as sedition by the Conclave. This is a culture that has mistaken institutional momentum for institutional health.
cultures.customs: Formality structures all public life. Greetings follow rank: inferiors address superiors with title and family name; equals exchange given names only after shared service or mutual obligation. Meals are communal in the lower tiers and private in the upper, so that eating alone in Aranthem is either a mark of poverty or power. The gesture of offering food — breaking bread and extending it — carries contractual weight: accepting food offered by an official implies acceptance of their jurisdiction. In the military, the sharing of a canteen is a field-trust ritual that predates the Conclave itself. Public mourning is forbidden after thirty days; grief beyond this period is considered a failure of civic duty. Art exists to commemorate, not to question. Music in the upper tiers is mathematical and precise; music in the lower tiers is mournful and ignored by anyone who could change the conditions that produced it. Oathbranding ceremonies are public spectacles — the disgraced officer is branded before their former unit, who are required to watch in silence. The brand's dissolution a year later is not ceremonial; it simply stops burning, and no one mentions it. This asymmetry — public disgrace, private absolution — reflects the empire's deepest cultural logic: shame is collective property; forgiveness is nobody's business.
cultures.socialStructure: Seven-tiered hierarchy corresponding to Aranthem's physical tiers, though the system extends throughout the empire as a conceptual framework even in towns with flat geography. First Tier (river districts, laborers, dockworkers) through Seventh Tier (Conclave members, archivists, high military). Social mobility exists but is glacial and primarily achieved through military service, administrative appointment, or marriage into a higher tier. The military is the most reliable ladder — a First Tier recruit who demonstrates competence can reach Fifth Tier within a career, which is how Kaelith rose before Calwrath. Women serve in all roles without formal restriction; the empire's gender politics are egalitarian in law and inconsistent in practice, meaning women command legions and govern provinces but are more harshly judged for failure. Same-sex partnerships carry no legal stigma — the empire recognized bonding contracts regardless of gender two centuries ago, when a Conclave member argued that restricting the tax base was fiscally irresponsible. The argument was administrative, not moral, which captures the empire's ethos perfectly. Inter-species relationships (human-fae) are not illegal but are regarded with deep social suspicion. The phrase 'Seep-touched' is used for humans who have had prolonged fae contact, implying contamination rather than connection. A bonding contract between a human and a fae would require Conclave approval that has never been granted. Class distinctions are expressed through dress (the number and quality of metal buttons — bone for the First Tier, copper through silver ascending, gold reserved for the Seventh), speech register (lower tiers use contractions and slang; upper tiers speak in complete sentences and passive constructions), and access to the Archive, which is theoretically public but practically restricted by a fee structure that prices out everyone below the Fourth Tier.
cultures.religion: The empire practices a polytheistic system called the Keeping — ostensibly the worship of the gods whose remnants are visible in the world, but functionally a bureaucratic framework for managing the divine. Six gods are acknowledged in the official canon: the Pallor's unnamed source (referred to only as the Sheltering One), Ythara the Unfinished (acknowledged as sealed and dangerous — prayers are offerings of completion, small finished objects placed at temple niches to 'satisfy' the god's nature), and four others whose names survive but whose worship has atrophied to the point where their temple niches in Veldrane and elsewhere stand empty. The Keeping does not demand faith. It demands observance: attend the quarterly Dimming ceremonies (when the Pallor is symbolically thanked for its protection), pay the temple tithe, register births and deaths at the parish temple. Priests are civil servants, not spiritual leaders — they maintain records, officiate contracts, and perform the Dimming rites with the practiced disinterest of people filing paperwork. The theology of the Keeping holds that gods are natural phenomena, like weather or geology, to be managed rather than worshipped. This pragmatic theology makes the empire extraordinarily vulnerable to what is actually happening: when the Pallor tears, the theological framework has no language for crisis, only for maintenance. The empire does not know how to pray because it never needed to. Deep reverence survives only in the border communities (Duskhaven, the Thornway garrisons) where proximity to the Greymere and the old marshlands preserved older, pre-Keeping folk practices: leaving milk at crossroads, speaking to the sea, refusing to name the dead before the thirtieth day.
taboos: Speaking to or acknowledging someone who bears an active Oathbrand in any context that implies social equality — they are erased from the tier system, existing below the First Tier as 'the Unranked.' Sharing a table with the Oathbranded is a social violation that implies either pity (condescending) or conspiracy (dangerous). Kaelith has spent five years being served last, seated alone, and addressed only when necessary.

Publicly questioning the integrity of the Pallor — the Conclave has made 'atmospheric speculation' a minor offense punishable by fine, which has the effect of making the tearing an open secret that no one discusses in spaces where they might be overheard.

Claiming to have seen true starlight — since the Pallor has been intact for centuries in the east, anyone who claims to have seen stars without the silver filter is either lying, mad, or has traveled to territories the empire does not want discussed.

Entering the Archive without sanctioned purpose — the knowledge housed there is controlled not through secrecy but through bureaucratic access restrictions. Knowledge is free in theory and inaccessible in practice.

Naming the dead within thirty days of death — a folk taboo that survives from pre-Keeping practice, still observed universally. The dead are referred to by relationship ('my mother,' 'the soldier,' 'the third one lost') until the Naming Day ceremony, when their name is spoken aloud for the last time and then belongs to the living again.

Fae-human physical intimacy — not legally prohibited but socially radioactive. The phrase 'sleeping with the current' references the Seep and implies a person has traded their loyalty, judgment, or identity for fae attention. The social penalty falls almost entirely on the human partner.

naming.givenNames: One to two syllables for common tiers, longer and more ornate ascending the hierarchy. Military names trend short and hard-consonant (Kaelith is notably unusual — a name from the eastern coast where the old pre-empire language survives in personal names, marking her as border-born even before her disgrace).
naming.familyNames: Indicate regional origin. '-en' suffix denotes eastern coastal (Maren — 'of the marsh' in the old tongue). '-eth' suffix denotes Aranthem birth. '-ane' suffix denotes the central provinces (Veldrane's naming influence). Family names are used in formal contexts; given names imply permission to use them.
naming.titles: Military ranks precede the name (Commander Maren, Lieutenant Dael — though fae ranks do not map cleanly). Conclave members are addressed as 'Seat [Family Name].' Oathbranded persons lose the right to their title permanently; they are addressed by given name only, which in a culture where given names imply intimacy creates an uncomfortable forced closeness — everyone must address Kaelith as if they know her, which means no one does.
naming.honorifics: Ser (gender-neutral, military or administrative respect), Keep (religious — rarely used outside formal Dimming ceremonies), Thresh (archaic, used only for the Oathbranded — literally 'threshold,' implying someone who exists between states).

cultures.name: Fae Culture — The Cerith Court and the Seep
cultures.description: A civilization that has survived by being extremely organized about cruelty. The fae are not whimsical, not charming, not mysterious in the human romantic sense — they are bureaucratic, precise, and cold in the way that a well-run prison is cold. The Cerith Court is simultaneously legislature, judiciary, and enforcement mechanism: it makes the contracts, interprets the contracts, and ensures the contracts are fulfilled. The Seep is its intelligence arm — 'the water that finds every crack' — operating across the boundary between fae territory and human lands. Fae culture is built on the foundational principle that binding agreements are the only thing that prevent chaos, because the fae remember the time before the Pallor, when the world was not yet finished and nothing could be relied upon. Their entire civilization is a response to the trauma of unfinished reality. They built order out of absolute, enforceable obligation. Love, in this framework, is not forbidden — it is simply not a recognized category. Affection is a private inefficiency, like a preference for one type of food over another. It has no contractual weight and therefore no cultural standing.
cultures.customs: All significant interactions are contractual. A fae greeting is an exchange of terms: 'I approach under the silence' (meaning: I am not here on Court business) or 'I approach under the weight' (meaning: I carry a contract). There is no casual greeting — every encounter is categorized. Meals are taken alone unless a sharing-contract has been established; eating with another fae implies a willingness to be bound by the conversation's content. Information is the primary currency — the Seep's operatives trade intelligence the way human merchants trade goods, with precise valuations and exchange rates. Fae mark the passage of time not in days or years but in 'turns' — the completion of a contract cycle. Lirien's age would be expressed as the number of contracts she has completed, not the years she has lived. Art exists as record-keeping: tapestries that are legal documents, songs that are precedent citations, sculptures that represent the physical dimensions of binding agreements. Beauty is an accident of precision, never a goal. The binding ceremony for a kill contract involves the operative drinking a preparation made from their own blood mixed with the target's — obtained through the Seep's intelligence apparatus. The contract becomes physically part of the operative; failure to complete it is not a legal penalty but a physiological one. Lirien carries Kaelith's blood in her veins. This is not metaphor.
cultures.socialStructure: The Cerith Court operates through three Orders: the Architects (who design contracts and policy), the Witnesses (who verify fulfillment and adjudicate disputes), and the Hands (who execute contracts — the category that includes Seep operatives like Lirien). Hierarchy within each Order is determined by contract completion rate and precision — a Hand who has completed forty-three assignments without deviation (Lirien) holds significant standing. There is no inherited status; the fae's rejection of 'potential' in favor of 'demonstrated capability' extends to their social organization. The Court's authority is absolute within fae territory and extends to any fae outside it through the binding mechanism of contracts. There is no concept of retirement — a fae serves until they choose to take the Unweaving, a voluntary dissolution that returns their essence to the collective. The Unweaving is not considered death; it is considered completion. Romantic or sexual partnerships exist but are classified as 'comfort arrangements' — private contracts between individuals that carry no social weight. They cannot interfere with Court obligations. A Hand whose comfort arrangement conflicts with an assigned contract is expected to dissolve the arrangement without negotiation. The fae have no concept of marriage as humans understand it — permanent emotional bonding is viewed as an ontological error, a confusion of preference with obligation. A fae who became genuinely, irrevocably attached to another being would be considered damaged — not morally, but structurally, the way a load-bearing wall with a crack is damaged. Lirien falling in love is not a transgression against fae law. It is, by fae understanding, a symptom of structural failure.
cultures.religion: The fae do not worship. They remember. Their spiritual practice is called the Reckoning — a communal recitation of the world as it was before the Pallor, before the god-sealing, when reality was not yet fixed and Ythara's nature was simply the way things were. The Reckoning is performed at the turning of each contract cycle and serves as a reminder of why the Court exists: because without binding, everything dissolves into potential. The fae view the Pallor not as protection but as a crude human solution to a problem the fae had already solved through contract. They consider the god-sealing an act of desperation by a species too disorganized to build proper containment. The Sheltering One (the Pallor's source god) is viewed with contempt — a deity who solved the problem of chaos by dying and becoming a ceiling. The fae attitude toward Ythara is complex: they fear the god's nature (unmade things, unrealized potential) because it represents exactly what their civilization was built to contain, but they do not hate it. Ythara is the condition that created the need for the Court. Without the god of unmade things, the fae would have no purpose. Some fae — quietly, carefully, in thoughts they would never speak — wonder whether the Court has become so invested in the necessity of containment that it would resist a genuine solution. The Seep, whose operatives work closest to the boundary, are most likely to have these thoughts. Lirien may be one of them.
taboos: Breaking a binding contract — not merely punished but physiologically impossible to survive. A fae who fails a kill contract does not face execution; the contract itself collapses inward, unmaking the parts of the fae that were bound to it. What remains is functional but incomplete. The Cerith Court calls them 'the Lessened.'

Expressing uncertainty in formal Court proceedings — ambiguity is an existential threat in a culture built on binding precision. The phrase 'I am not certain' in a Court context is treated as a confession of structural inadequacy.

Forming attachments that supersede contractual obligations — not illegal per se, but treated as a form of cognitive impairment. A fae operative who reported feelings for a target would be reassigned, evaluated, and possibly subjected to Recalibration — a process of contract-reinforcement that rewrites the operative's priorities at a level deeper than conscious choice.

Sharing fae food with humans — not because of magical contamination (a human myth) but because meals are contractual spaces, and sharing food with an unbindable being creates a contract with no enforceable second party. The fae consider it conceptually obscene, like signing a document with blank space where the cosignatory should be.

Speaking a fae's contract-name in human territory — each fae has a public name (used among humans) and a contract-name (used in Court proceedings). The contract-name carries binding weight; speaking it outside fae jurisdiction creates an unmoored obligation that attaches to whatever is nearby. 'Lirien Dael' is a cover name, shaped to sound natural in human language. Her contract-name is something she has never spoken aloud outside the Court.

Returning home without contract fulfillment — the boundary between fae territory and the human world is not physical but contractual. A fae under active contract who approaches the boundary without completion is simply unable to cross. The boundary recognizes unfulfilled obligation the way a locked door recognizes the absence of a key.

naming.pattern: Public name (for human interaction) + Contract-name (Court use only)
naming.publicNames: Constructed to be pronounceable in human language while retaining fae phonetic markers — liquid consonants (l, r), open vowels, and a two-part structure that mimics human given-name + family-name format. 'Lirien Dael' is designed to pass in human society while containing fae phonetic identity: 'Lirien' carries the lir- root (meaning 'current' or 'flow,' associating her with the Seep), 'Dael' is a simplification that suggests lineage without revealing the actual contract-line.
naming.contractNames: Polysyllabic, precise, and never shared with outsiders. Contract-names encode the fae's Order, contract-line, completion history, and current binding status. They are spoken in full only during Court proceedings and binding ceremonies. A contract-name is not a secret — it is a weapon. Speaking it gives the listener a thread of binding access to the named fae.
naming.titles: Functional rather than honorific: Hand (operative), Architect (designer), Witness (adjudicator). Within the Seep, operatives carry operation-names for specific assignments — Lirien's designation for the Kaelith mission is not given but would be a water-related term following the Seep's naming convention.
naming.honorifics: None. The fae do not use honorifics because all status is derived from demonstrated contract history, which is known to all parties without needing verbal signaling. You do not call a senior fae 'sir' because their standing is already visible in the way other fae position themselves relative to them. Hierarchy is expressed through spatial arrangement, not language.

cultures.name: Eastern Border Culture — The Garrison Coast
cultures.description: The culture of Duskhaven, Calwrath (before its destruction), and the string of coastal garrison towns along the empire's northeastern edge. This is military culture in atrophy — communities built for purpose that the empire quietly withdrew, leaving the infrastructure of war without the war to justify it. The people here are the empire's afterthought: soldiers posted and forgotten, civilians who service a garrison economy that no longer needs servicing, and fisherfolk whose relationship with the sea predates the empire by centuries. The Pallor sits heaviest here, thick and low, and the culture reflects it — a permanent twilight sensibility, superstitious, practical, and darkly humorous. These are people who expect nothing and are rarely disappointed.
cultures.customs: The garrison coast retains military timekeeping — meals at bell-marks, curfew traditions that no commanding officer enforces but everyone observes, the morning muster that became a community gathering after the troops thinned. Wall maintenance is communal and near-sacred: every able adult in Duskhaven takes a rotation repairing the seawall and the garrison fortifications, not because they expect attack but because the act of maintaining is the closest thing to meaning the town has. Kaelith's compulsive wall-fixing is not eccentric here — it is the most normal thing she does. Fishing customs carry pre-Keeping superstitions: the first catch is returned to the sea with a whispered apology (not a prayer — the fisher is sorry for the taking, not grateful for the gift), nets are mended only in daylight (darkness tangles the weave), and no fisher names their boat after a living person. The Listing Mast — the tavern where Kaelith drinks — follows garrison tradition: a nail is driven into the doorframe for each year the town has gone without action. The doorframe is so thick with nails that it looks furred. Drinking culture is functional, not celebratory — alcohol is warmth, social lubricant, and anesthetic. The coast's humor is dry to the point of desiccation: irony is the default register, sincerity is suspect, and the worst insult is to take something seriously that doesn't deserve it.
cultures.socialStructure: Technically the garrison towns follow imperial tier structure, but in practice the hierarchy is flattened by isolation and shared irrelevance. The garrison commander is nominally Fifth Tier but is treated as a neighbor who happens to have a title. The harbormaster holds more practical authority than any military officer. Social status derives from usefulness: the healer, the smith, the net-mender, and the woman who knows which paths through the Greymere are safe at which tides all outrank anyone whose contribution is merely administrative. The Oathbranded are treated differently here than in the heartland — not with social warmth, but with the pragmatic tolerance of a community that cannot afford to waste functional people. Kaelith is not welcomed in Duskhaven, but she is used. She fixes walls. She carries supplies. She is strong and competent and available. The garrison coast's attitude toward her is not kindness — it is efficiency that looks like kindness if you do not examine it too closely, which is its own wound.
cultures.religion: The Keeping's formal structures exist — there is a temple in Duskhaven with a single overworked priest who performs the Dimming ceremonies and registers births and deaths — but the living religious practice on the coast is the older, pre-Keeping folk tradition. The sea is addressed directly, not as a god but as a force that requires acknowledgment. Crossroads receive milk or salt offerings. The dead are watched for thirty days not because of theological requirement but because 'they need time to decide whether they are really leaving.' The Greymere is considered a liminal space where the rules of the world thin — not a place of evil, but a place of uncertainty, and the coast's relationship with uncertainty is one of resigned coexistence rather than fear. The Pallor is understood locally as something between a roof and a wound — it protects, yes, but something had to be hurt to make it, and the coast people feel that debt more viscerally than the heartland where the theology has been sanitized into bureaucracy.
taboos: Whistling at night — draws attention from things in the Greymere that are better left unnoticed. Not a superstition the garrison command acknowledges, but every soldier posted to Duskhaven learns it within the first week.

Mentioning the Siege of Calwrath to Kaelith or in her presence — not because the town has decided she was wrong, but because they have decided the topic produces nothing useful. The coast's pragmatism extends to conversation: if talking about something cannot change it, talking about it is waste.

Refusing shared labor — declining a wall-repair rotation or a net-mending session without physical incapacity is the closest thing to exile the coast offers. Community survival requires participation; opting out is opting out of the community.

Asking a stranger their business — garrison coast etiquette holds that a person's reason for being somewhere is their own affair until it becomes the community's problem. This is why Lirien can arrive in Duskhaven and receive lodging and food without interrogation, but also why she is watched with careful, undemonstrative attention from the moment she sets foot in town.

naming.givenNames: Short, often one syllable, with harder consonants than heartland names. The eastern coast preserves pre-empire naming roots: 'Kael-' roots (meaning 'edge' or 'boundary' in the old tongue), 'Bren-' roots (meaning 'burn' or 'fire'), 'Thar-' roots (meaning 'stone'). Names are chosen for sound and practicality, not aspiration.
naming.familyNames: The '-en' suffix dominates (Maren, Thoren, Carren), indicating marsh or coastal origin. Family names often derive from geographic features: Maren ('of the marsh'), Greyen ('of the grey'), Salthen ('of the salt').
naming.titles: Military ranks are used with declining formality. 'Commander' has become a near-ironic term — Duskhaven's garrison commander is addressed as 'Commander' the way a shopkeeper might be addressed as 'Your Majesty' in jest. The title carries its weight only when the person bearing it has earned it through visible competence, not appointment.
naming.honorifics: None used locally. The coast considers honorifics a heartland affectation.

cultures.name: The Wending Communities
cultures.description: Loose networks of forest-dwellers who have lived under the canopy of the Wending for generations, never accepting the High Conclave's authority and never needing to — the forest predates the empire by millennia and will outlast it by more. These are not primitive people; they are people who made a different choice about what to organize around. Where the empire organized around territory and the fae around contracts, the Wending communities organized around the forest itself — its rhythms, its growth patterns, its relationship with the Pallor (which is barely visible under the canopy, making the Wending one of the few places in the known world where the sky does not feel like a ceiling). They are herbalists, woodworkers, scholars of living systems, and the keepers of knowledge the empire's Archive either never contained or quietly lost.
cultures.customs: The Wending communities practice a form of rotating habitation — settlements shift location every few years as the forest's growth patterns change, following the canopy's guidance rather than imposing permanent structures. Homes are built into living trees using techniques that do not kill or wound the host — a house is a negotiation with its tree, and a tree that rejects the negotiation by growing through the structure is allowed to win. The metaphor is intentional: the Wending people believe that living things cannot be owned, only partnered with, and that partnership requires ongoing consent. Meals are foraged and communal, with a rotating host system — the family that feeds the community this season is fed by the community the next. Visitors are welcome but must observe the Canopy Rule: take only what grows below your eye level, speak below the volume that startles birds, walk on existing paths. The forest teaches patience, and the Wending communities have learned. Their councils make decisions by consensus, which means decisions happen slowly — a fact that has frustrated every imperial administrator sent to 'integrate' them and eventually convinced the empire to stop trying. Storytelling is the primary art form: histories are spoken, not written, and a skilled speaker can hold a community for hours with a tale that is understood to be simultaneously literal and metaphorical. The Wending people are the most likely to have oral histories of the god-war, the Pallor's creation, and the original path to the Quietus — information that the Archive may have lost or suppressed.
cultures.socialStructure: No fixed hierarchy. Authority is situational: the best healer leads during illness, the best pathfinder leads during travel, the best speaker leads during council. Permanent leadership is considered a form of arrogance — the assumption that one person's judgment is reliable across all contexts. Age is respected but not automatically deferred to; the Wending saying is 'An old fool is a fool who survived.' Gender is understood fluidly — the Wending communities recognize a spectrum rather than a binary, and relationship structures vary from person to person without formal classification. The concept of restricting partnership by gender strikes Wending people as bizarre, like restricting partnership by hair color. They do not have a word for sexual orientation because they do not have the concept of orientation as a category. This makes the Wending communities the only culture in the story where Kaelith and Lirien's relationship — two women, one human, one fae — would provoke no reaction at all. The interspecies aspect might raise curiosity but not judgment. The forest has hosted stranger partnerships.
cultures.religion: The Wending communities practice what they call Listening — a meditative attention to the forest's signals that blurs the line between spiritual practice and ecological science. They do not worship the forest; they pay attention to it, which the Wending consider a more honest form of devotion. The Pallor is barely relevant to their daily lives — under the canopy, the silver membrane is an abstraction. But where the Pallor has torn above the Wending's heart, the forest has changed, and the communities are the first people in the story to have a nuanced response to Ythara's influence: not terror, not worship, but careful observation. The trees growing in spirals, the flowers blooming in two states — the Wending note these changes the way a farmer notes an early frost: as information that requires adaptation, not as apocalypse. Their oral histories contain references to the time before the Pallor that align with neither the empire's theology (gods as natural phenomena to be managed) nor the fae Reckoning (reality before binding). The Wending account suggests that the world before the Pallor was not chaos — it was simply different, and the decision to seal it under silver was made by beings who preferred their own version of reality to whatever it was becoming.
taboos: Cutting living wood without speaking to the tree first — this is not animism in the human theological sense; the Wending do not believe trees have human-type consciousness. They believe that living things have momentum, and interrupting momentum without acknowledgment creates imbalance.

Raising one's voice above the canopy threshold — shouting is considered a form of violence against the forest's acoustic ecology. Conflicts are resolved in whispers, which has the incidental effect of making Wending disagreements far more intense than shouting matches, because quiet rage has nowhere to dissipate.

Claiming to own land — the concept is linguistically impossible in the Wending dialect, which has no possessive form for non-portable objects. You can own a knife (which you carry) but not a clearing (which carries you).

Refusing hospitality to travelers — the Canopy Rule grants safe passage to anyone willing to observe it. Turning away a traveler who follows the rules is equivalent to claiming the forest serves your preferences rather than its own.

naming.pattern: Personal name + Tree-name (the species of tree the person was born under or nearest to)
naming.personalNames: Drawn from natural observations: sounds, textures, seasonal events. Names are chosen by the community, not the parents, typically on the third day after birth when the nearest tree is identified and consulted (by a community elder who sits with the tree for a full day, reading its bark patterns and growth direction).
naming.treeNames: Function as lineage markers — not biological family but ecological context. Two people born under the same oak species share a tree-name, creating a network of kinship that is geographic and seasonal rather than genetic.
naming.titles: Situational only: 'Speaker' (for the person currently holding council authority), 'Pathfinder' (for guides), 'Listener' (for those in meditative practice). These titles are held temporarily and relinquished when the context changes.
naming.honorifics: None. The Wending consider fixed honorifics a symptom of cultures that have forgotten how to pay attention to individuals.

cultures.name: Rivenmarch Freeholders
cultures.description: The people who chose distance over order — a scattered population living in the ontologically unstable territory between the Vireth Range and the Unwalked Reach, where the empire's maps claim sovereignty and its authority ends. Rivenmarch freeholders are woodcutters, herders, hedge-witches, deserters, scholars who found the Archive's politics intolerable, and the descendants of all of these. They are not a unified culture so much as a shared condition: people who have adapted to living in a place where the ground shifts, the weather contradicts itself, and the distinction between what is and what could be grows thinner every year. They are practical, superstitious, deeply self-reliant, and possessed of a black humor that makes the garrison coast seem sunny by comparison. If the empire's culture is 'things have always worked,' the Rivenmarch ethos is 'things have never worked, and we're still here.'
cultures.customs: Architecture is impermanent by design — wood and thatch structures that can be rebuilt in days when the ground shifts or a field that was solid last week decides to become a pond. Permanence is considered not just impractical but philosophically naive. The freeholders mark their settlements with cairns that are rebuilt monthly — the act of rebuilding is the point, not the structure. This monthly cairn-raising is the closest thing to communal ritual the Rivenmarch has: everyone gathers, stacks stones, shares food, and does not discuss whether the stones will still be there next month. Barter is the primary economy — imperial currency is accepted but distrusted because its value depends on an authority that does not reach here. The most valued trade goods are things that remain what they are: salt (always salt), iron (always iron), and preserved food (always food). 'Staying power' is the highest compliment you can pay a thing or a person in the Rivenmarch — the quality of remaining yourself regardless of external pressure. Trade caravans from the empire are received with cautious hospitality; fae travelers are treated with the same wariness applied to weather — respected, tracked, and not argued with.
cultures.socialStructure: Radically flat. There are no formal leaders, no councils, no governing structures. Disputes are resolved through direct negotiation between affected parties; if negotiation fails, the community separates — one party moves to a different part of the Rivenmarch. Land cannot be owned (it changes too much to be claimed), so property disputes do not exist. Social standing derives entirely from reliability: can this person be counted on when the ground shifts? Have they stayed when others left? The ultimate mark of respect is having survived multiple 'shifts' — the Rivenmarch term for the ontological changes that periodically reorganize the landscape. Newcomers are neither welcomed nor rejected; they are observed for a season and then either absorbed or not, based on whether they adapt. The freeholders are the only human culture that has any familiarity with fae — not through diplomatic contact but through proximity. The Seep has used the Rivenmarch as a transit corridor for generations, and occasional fae travelers pass through. The freeholders' response is pragmatic indifference: fae are people with different rules, like neighbors with different customs. They are not feared or revered. They are, at most, noted.
cultures.religion: The Rivenmarch freeholders practice what they call Grounding — a set of daily habits designed to reinforce the reality of the self in a landscape that undermines certainty. Each morning, a freeholder touches iron, drinks water from a vessel they filled themselves, and states their own name aloud. This is not prayer; it is calibration. The practice exists because Ythara's influence makes identity feel provisional — a freeholder who spends too long near the Reach without Grounding begins to feel as if they could be someone else, as if their history is one version among many. The freeholders have no theology, no gods, no priests. They believe in the persistence of material things (iron stays iron, salt stays salt, a person who remembers their name stays that person) and they organize their spiritual life around reinforcing those anchors. They are aware of both the Keeping and the fae Reckoning and consider both to be overly complicated responses to a simple problem: the world is not stable, so you must be. The freeholders' relationship with Ythara is the most intimate of any culture — they live in the god's influence daily, and they have developed not fear but a working relationship. When the ground shifts, they rebuild. When a field changes, they plant something new. When the rivers pause, they wait. They have no name for what Ythara is because naming it would imply it is finished, which contradicts its nature.
taboos: Asking someone why they came to the Rivenmarch — the answer is always some version of 'because I left somewhere else,' and the details are private unless volunteered. Many freeholders are deserters, exiles, or fugitives; the Rivenmarch's social contract is that your past is irrelevant as long as your present is reliable.

Building in stone — permanence is not just impractical but culturally offensive. Stone buildings imply a belief that the world will stay still for you, which the Rivenmarch has disproven repeatedly. The one exception is cairns, which are rebuilt monthly and therefore technically temporary.
Wasting salt or iron — both are ontological anchors, substances that resist Ythara's influence. Using either carelessly is treated with the same gravity as wasting water in a desert.

Speaking for someone who is present — in a culture where identity is actively maintained against dissolution, representing another person's thoughts or feelings is considered a form of erasure. Each person speaks for themselves, always, even children.

naming.pattern: Chosen name only — no family name, no lineage
naming.chosenNames: Freeholders choose their own names upon arrival or upon their first 'shift' survival. Many adopt names from the old pre-empire tongues; others take descriptive names from the landscape. Some keep their birth names — the act of choosing to retain a name you were given is as significant as choosing a new one. Names are short and grounded, favoring hard consonants and single syllables: Tarn, Keth, Bracken, Rust.
naming.familyNames: Deliberately absent. Lineage implies a past that claims authority over the present, which contradicts Rivenmarch philosophy. If pressed, a freeholder might identify themselves by settlement location: 'Keth, Ridgeside' — but this is direction-giving, not identity.
naming.titles: None. The Rivenmarch has no titles because titles imply fixed roles, and the landscape does not permit fixity.
naming.honorifics: The only quasi-honorific is 'Stayed' — used to describe someone who has survived in the Rivenmarch for more than five years. 'She's Stayed' means she has proven her persistence against a landscape that tests it daily. It is the highest respect the freeholders offer.

socialRules: IMPERIAL: Oathbranded persons are socially erased — they exist below the tier system, cannot hold property, cannot be addressed by title, and cannot initiate formal interactions. They persist at the tolerance of whatever community houses them. This is Kaelith's daily reality and the first thing Lirien must navigate: how to interact with a person the culture has made invisible without either drawing attention or participating in the erasure.

IMPERIAL: Fae visitors within empire territory must register at garrison checkpoints and carry transit seals validated by the Conclave. Fae traveling without documentation are assumed to be Seep operatives and subject to detention. Lirien's cover identity must include forged transit documentation, which limits her movement to routes with corrupt or understaffed checkpoints.

IMPERIAL: Inter-species romantic or sexual relationships carry no legal penalty but extreme social stigma. The human partner is considered 'Seep-touched' — compromised, suspect, unreliable. The fae partner faces no stigma from human society (humans have no leverage over fae behavior) but would face scrutiny from the Cerith Court for forming attachments that could compromise contracts. Both women risk social destruction from their own cultures if their relationship becomes known.

IMPERIAL: Public speech about the Pallor's deterioration is fined. Private speech about it is universal. This creates a culture of whispered consensus — everyone knows the sky is failing, no one is permitted to say so officially, and the gap between public and private truth has become so wide that trust in any institutional statement has evaporated. Characters who speak plainly about the Pallor are either very brave or very desperate.

FAE: A fae operative under active contract exists in a state of total obligation — personal desires, relationships, and preferences are contractually subordinate to the mission. Lirien's growing feelings for Kaelith do not violate fae law (feelings are not legislated) but they compromise her operational integrity, which in fae culture is worse than breaking a rule. A compromised Hand is a structural risk to every contract that depends on the network's reliability.

FAE: The boundary between fae territory and the human world enforces unfulfilled contracts — Lirien literally cannot go home until Kaelith is dead or the contract is dissolved, and contract dissolution requires a Cerith Court proceeding that the Court has no incentive to grant. This is not a metaphorical prison; it is a physical one defined by obligation rather than walls.

FAE: Fae do not lie. This is not a moral principle — it is a contractual one. The Cerith Court's binding mechanism depends on precision of language, and a fae who lies introduces ambiguity into the system. Instead, fae practice radical omission: they will tell you exactly what they choose to tell you and nothing more, and the absence of information is not considered deception. Lirien does not lie to Kaelith about the kill contract — she simply never mentions it, and by fae standards, this is honesty.

WENDING: The Canopy Rule grants safe passage to any traveler who observes its terms: take only what grows below eye level, speak below the volume that startles birds, walk on existing paths. This provides Kaelith and Lirien safe haven in the Wending but requires them to adopt the forest's pace — no rushing, no forcing, no taking more than you need. The forest teaches patience, which is exactly what the slow-burn romance requires at this stage of the journey.

RIVENMARCH: No one asks your history, no one claims authority, no one speaks for anyone else. This radical autonomy is the environment where TP-3 (chosen versus only) is tested: in the Rivenmarch, every institutional constraint that forced Kaelith and Lirien together has no reach. They are free to separate. They do not. The culture of the Rivenmarch reinforces this by not caring either way — unlike the empire (which would judge them) or the Cerith Court (which would assess them), the Rivenmarch simply observes.

CROSS-CULTURAL: The journey from east to west is also a journey from cultural rigidity to cultural fluidity — from the empire's tier system and formal taboos through the Wending's flexible consensus to the Rivenmarch's radical impermanence. This gradient mirrors the Pallor gradient and the romance arc: as external structures dissolve, internal choices become more visible. The question of whether Kaelith and Lirien choose each other is sharpest in the places where nothing external forces them together.

CROSS-CULTURAL: Naming carries cultural information. In the empire, Kaelith's name marks her as border-born (eastern coastal roots); among the fae, Lirien's public name marks her as Seep-affiliated. In the Wending, names describe ecological context. In the Rivenmarch, names are chosen and personal. The progression of naming conventions across the journey mirrors the protagonists' identity evolution: from names imposed by institutional context to names that are chosen and personal.

CROSS-CULTURAL: Food-sharing is significant in every culture but means different things — imperial food-sharing implies jurisdictional acceptance, fae food-sharing implies contractual willingness, Wending food-sharing implies community membership, Rivenmarch food-sharing implies trust in the food's 'staying power.' When Kaelith and Lirien share a meal, the meaning shifts with geography, and the chapter drafter should be aware of which cultural frame applies.
